findings.detail: Six nodes (D1, D2, D3, C1, C2, C3) in two direction-TB subgraphs under a flowchart LR root. All bold labels are 2–4 words. Every label traces directly to §5 Core Concepts: 'def keyword' (corpus: 'def keyword defines the function'), 'Function Name' (corpus: 'Name / Body (indented code) / Call'), 'Body stored in memory' (corpus: 'stores it in memory, does NOT run it'), 'Caller line' (corpus: 'Function call (name+()) actually runs the body'), 'Body executes' (corpus: 'call triggers execution'), 'Returns to caller' (corpus: 'execution flow back to caller'). No downstream topics (parameters, return values, lists, classes) appear. Title 'Definition vs Call — two phases of every function' sits in the config title: field — correct per title-discipline rule. Each subgraph is a clean single-path linear flow (no branching). DEFINE-->CALL cross-subgraph edge represents the phase transition, not a branch. classDef tiers: start (blue #a5d8ff), auto (purple #d0bfff), act (blue #a5d8ff, matches SKILL definition), done (green #b2f2bb) — all correct. No ghost nodes. No unexplained acronyms. Subgraph backgrounds differentiated (blue-tinted DEFINE, green-tinted CALL) for visual phase separation.

Side-by-side panel layout (LR root + direction-TB subgraphs) exactly matches the diagram intent: 'illustrating the definition-vs-call distinction visually' with 'TWO phases'. Follows the canonical 2-4 golden-dataset pattern. DEFINE-->CALL edge with label 'call triggers execution' makes the phase transition explicit and beginner-readable.
